Some councils such as Westminster council in London do not enforce single yellow lines on public holidays however Kensington and Chelsea do,

In 2009 boxing day was on a Saturday. Many councils said that it wasn’t a public holiday because the actual public holiday was on the following Monday. Many people who visited their relatives or went shopping received parking tickets which we think is disgraceful. In 2010 New years day is on a Saturday! On boxing day 2009 one of the motorists who received a penalty charge notice on a pay and display bay in Kensington and Chelsea appealed. See case below.

Case 2100046537 in Kensington and Chelsea.

On Boxing day 26th December 2009 a motorist parked on a pay and display bay. The signs said “free parking in pay and display bays on Sunday, Public and Bank holidays” He understood this to mean he could park for free on Boxing day Saturday 26th December 2009. The council argued that the bank holiday was the following Monday. The adjudicator said that he found the words on the pay and display machine “sufficiently ambiguous to confuse a motorist when parking on Boxing day Saturday the 26th November” and allowed the appeal. He also said that “if the authority wishes to enforce charges on Boxing day which falls on a Saturday the pay and display machine should clearly state so”

We say it is a disgrace that councils seek to profit from confused motorists during the festive season.

Gwendolyn Zammitt vs. The London Borough of Greenwich

On 5th April 1996 Mrs Zammitt parked her motor car in Chalton Church Lane, Greenwich.  It was a public holiday being Good Friday.  She did not think she had parked illegally because Good Friday was a public holiday.  Mrs Zammitt appealed to the parking adjudicator who rejected her appeal.

Mr G Krisovic vs the London Borough of Hammersmith and Fulham

This is the case of Mr Kirsovic who parked his car on a bank holiday in Hammersmith and Fulham and received a PCN.  He had assumed (like thousands of other motorists) that waiting restrictions do not apply on bank holidays although unless there is a specific confirmation shown on the signs or information shown on a pay and display machine then parking restrictions apply as on any other day.  His appeal was dismissed.

sections.heading: 24: CCTV Enforcement
sections.content:
October 11th 2011.
Mr Robert Francis Hepworth vs Westminster City Council
This is one of 18 cases where Westminster council have lost at appeal as their cameras are not certified for use by the department of transport. In the week ending 12 November 2011 there were 16 further appeals allowed by the parking adjudicator against Westminster on this point. See our CCTV section for more details.
Westminster Council’s parking cameras are confirmed as not approved devices. The legally required certificate from the department of transport authorising their use for parking enforcement refers to a different camera model. See our news section. The full decision is below.Please note that there have been furtehr cases where the council have won.
Register Kept Under Regulation 20 of the Road Traffic (Parking Adjudicators)(London) Regulations 1993, as amended or Paragraph 21 of the Schedule to the Civil Enforcement of Parking Contraventions (England) Representations and Appeals Regulations 2007, as applicable
Case Reference: 2110348237
Appellant: Mr Robert Francis Hepworth
Authority: Westminster
VRM: LT57LMY
PCN: WM68224298
Contravention Date: 28 May 2011
Contravention Time: 08:06
Contravention Location: Prince Consort Road
Penalty Amount: £130.00
Contravention: Stopped on a pedestrian crossing or zig-zags
Decision Date: 11 Oct 2011
Adjudicator: Andrew Harman
Appeal Decision: Allowed
Direction: cancel the Penalty Charge Notice and the Notice to Owner.
Reasons: This appeal was scheduled for personal hearing on 13 September 2011.
The council did not attend and was not represented.
The appellant attended represented by Mr Wise.
The appellant’s case was set out in Mr Wise’s detailed written submissions he reading out those submissions at the hearing.
Because those papers had not been served upon the council I adjourned the appeal for three weeks asking the council to respond to the points made if enforcement was being pursued telling Mr Wise that the matter would be then be relisted before me for postal decision.
The matter was in fact adjourned for a further week on the application of the council.
I note on the appeal being relisted before me today that the council has through solicitors made submissions as set out in its e-mails of 26 September 2011, with enclosure, and 7 October 2011, with enclosures.
I have not been provided with any additional papers or evidence by the council.
I am satisfied although I note Mr Wise’s application on the point that given my decision in this case there is no necessity for a further oral hearing.
Although Mr Wise makes a number of submissions I am not required to consider all issues raised by a party to the proceedings but only those necessary to determine the appeal.
If I am satisfied that a ground of appeal advanced by an appellant has been satisfied it is not then necessary for me to consider whether any other ground might also be satisfied.
I consider first whether the council has proved the contravention.
Evidence of this incident was gathered by cctv camera.
The council submits that its cctv camera model is certified by the Secretary of State providing  VCA Certificate PAD038 in support.  Its approved devices are set out in its document Appendix A – Cameras, on which it identifies with an asterisk the model number of the camera it states was used to gather evidence of this contravention
Mr Wise submits in his “2nd Ground of Appeal”, (pages 71-72 on the PATAS evidence tree), that although VCA Certificate PAD038 certifies a camera type DVTel9840 that is not the camera type deployed at the location. The camera deployed he states is in fact listed in the council’s Appendix A as being camera type DVTel 9840A which Mr Wise submits is a variant of the camera type certified as an approved device.
In the absence of any explanation on the point by the council I am satisfied that this must be  so and cannot for that reason be satisfied that the camera used is as required a device certified as an approved device by the Secretary of State.  I am not therefore satisfied that evidence of this contravention has been gathered by an approved device and thus find that the contravention has not been proved.
I make no finding in respect of any other issue raised by either party to the proceedings but for the reason given the appeal is allowed.
There have been further cases however where the council have won
Bell VS Southwark
In the case of Bell Vs London Borough of Southwark, a penalty charge notice had been issued by post for a parking contravention. The appellant stated that he had not received the PCN even though it had been posted by the council. Although the adjudicator said that where legislation provides for the service of a document by post service was deemed to have been effected 2 working days after the document was posted, he said this presumption was rebuttable. The local authority proceeded to issue a notice to owner because they had not received payment for the penalty, but the adjudicator found that they were not entitled to serve a notice to owner because the penalty charge notice had not been properly ‘issued’ under the 1991 act. He allowed the motorists appeal on the basis that the motorist had not received the penalty charge notice posted by the council.
Wirral – The following case which is reproduced in full is important as the national adjudicator refers to the statutory guidance which the London Adjudicator’s sometimes seem to dismiss as “just guidance” We disagree as the guidance is “statutory” and councils “must have regard to it”
Case Number WL 05219F
Page 1 of 2
Adjudicator’s Decision
Rachel Johnson
and
Metropolitan Borough of Wirral
Penalty Charge Notice WL50002776 £70.00
Appeal allowed on the ground that the contravention did not occur and
there was procedural impropriety on the part of the Council.
I direct the Council to cancel the Penalty Charge Notice.
Reasons
The PCN was issued in relation to a contravention on 16 April 2009 at 10.47 by vehicle Y784 GMA, of being parked in a restricted street during prescribed hours. This case was listed for hearing by telephone on 14 July 2009 when Mr Hooper on behalf of Miss Johnson and Mr Parsons on behalf of the Council, gave evidence. Mr Hooper stated that he parked the vehicle on double yellow lines, in order to collect a large quantity of cash and deliver heavy bags of change to his adjacent shop premises. The Traffic Regulation Order contains exemptions from the “no waiting” restrictions for vehicles which are being used, for so long as may be necessary, to unload goods (Article 6) or to deliver cash to adjacent premises (Article 4F). “Goods” are defined by the Order as meaning: “heavy or bulky articles”.
The Council have supplied the signed statement of a camera operator but the statement does not include the operator’s name. The Department for Transport issued Guidance to local authorities in March 2008 (the Guidance). At paragraph 8.78 the Guidance states: “Motorists may regard enforcement by cameras as over-zealous and authorities should use them sparingly. The Secretary of State recommends that authorities put up signs to tell drivers that they are using cameras to detect contraventions. Signs must comply with TSRGD or have special authorisation from DfT. The Secretary of State recommends that approved devices are used only where enforcement is difficult or sensitive and CEO enforcement is not practical. Approved devices should not be used where permits or exemptions (such as resident permits or Blue Badges) not visible to the equipment may apply”.
Case Number WL 05219F
Page 2 of 2
Furthermore, at paragraph 8.82: “An essential and integral part of any system is a code of practice. This sets out the objectives of the system and the rules it will follow. Authorities should ensure that they produce (or adopt) and follow a code of practice. The code should make sure that staff deal properly with issues such as privacy, integrity and fairness. It should set minimum standards to ensure public confidence in the scheme.”
Mr Parsons could not confirm the contents of the Council’s code of practice for camera enforcement. He stated that if a disabled badge was displayed in a vehicle this could be checked “on foot”.
I find that:
(a) the vehicle was parked only for so long as necessary to deliver/collect heavy change and cash in the course of a business;
(b) the Council have not supplied evidence that: adequate signs were in place on the contravention date warning motorists of camera enforcement;
(c) camera enforcement is not appropriate at this location. This appeal is allowed on the ground that the contravention did not occur and there was procedural impropriety on the part of the Council.
David Binns
Adjudicator 24 July 2009
Sainsbury’s supermarkets VS Camden Council
This is an extremely important case as clearly the council have not “had regard to” the guidance issued by the secretary of state in respect of CCTV enforcement. (Details can be found in our CCTV section) Sometimes the adjudicators just say “it is just guidance” but here they have allowed an appeal soley on the basis that the council did not follow the guidance and should not have used CCTV. CCTV should only be used where CEO (civil enforcement officer) enforcement is not practical or where it is dangerous to use CEO enforcement. Neither was the case at this location. In fact pretty much all CCTV parking tickets issued by Local authorities are actually issued on the same roads where they have traffic wardens patroling so it is clearly not impractical to use them! We have copied the decision in full.
Register Kept Under Regulation 20 of the Road Traffic
(Parking Adjudicators)(London) Regulations 1993, as amended or Paragraph 21 of the Schedule to the Civil Enforcement of Parking Contraventions (England) Representations and Appeals Regulations 2007, as applicable
PATAS Logo
Case Reference: 211001669A
Appellant: Sainsburys Supermarkets
Authority: Camden
VRM: KX55OGU
PCN: CU28412516
Contravention Date: 03 Dec 2010
Contravention Time: 22:56
Contravention Location:Tottenham Court Road
Penalty Amount: Â£120.00
Contravention: Stopped on a restricted bus stop
Decision Date: 15 Feb 2011
Adjudicator: Hugh Cooper
Appeal Decision: Allowed
Direction: cancel the Penalty Charge Notice.
Mr Janssen appeared before me today to represent the Appellants. He does not dispute that that the Appellants’ vehicle was stopped on a bus
stop. He stated that this was for only a short time, and that he had been informed that this was a new driver who had stopped briefly to look at his
directions.
However he reiterated the argument put forward in his representations and Notice of Appeal, to the effect that in using CCTV enforcement at this
location the Enforcement Authority had failed to have regard to the Secretary of State’s guidance, which was in breach of the mandatory requirement to do so contained in Section 87 of the Traffic Management Act 2004 (TMA). In his Notice of Appeal he cited the terms of Section 87
of the TMA and of the part of the Guidance to the effect that the Secretary of State recommends that approved devices are used “only where
enforcement is difficult or sensitive and CEO enforcement is not practical”.
He argued that were this a dual carriageway with no pavements or safe place for a Civil Enforcement Officer (CEO) camera enforcement would
be appropriate, but that it is clear that CEO enforcement is entirely possible in Tottenham Court Road. He submitted at the hearing that the
Authority had produced no evidence to show that they had in fact had regard to the Secretary of State’s guidance.
In response to Mr Janssen’s argument the Authority wrote in their Notice of Rejection, “You explain that use of CCTV enforcement at this location
Statutory Register Entry Page 1 of 3
Reasons:
is contrary to guidance issued by the Secretary of State For Transport. I should however explain that we take a different view here and that if you wish to take this matter further you will need to make use of the appeal form provided to take the case to the independent adjudicator”.
In their Case Summary the Authority cited the terms of Regulation 10 of the Civil Enforcement of Parking Contraventions (England) General Regulations 2007, which allows a Enforcement Authority to serve a PCN by post on the basis of a record produced by an approved device.
Section 87 of the TMA clearly does not impose a mandatory duty on Local Authorities to follow any guidance published by the appropriate national authority, but it does impose a duty to have regard to such guidance. The phrase ‘must have regard to’ imposes a positive obligation on Local Authorities to think about the guidance. This obligation is balanced by a discretion to determine what weight they should give to the guidance in any particular location or set of circumstances. When making a decision, in this case as to whether to employ camera enforcement at this location, they must be able to demonstrate clearly that they applied their mind to the guidance, even though ultimately they chose not to follow it. The Authority in this case do not claim that enforcement is difficult or sensitive or that CEO enforcement is not practical at this location, so there
is no obvious reason why the guidelines should not have been followed, and enforcement carried out by CEOs rather than by camera. However the Authority have not given any reasons as to why they nevertheless consider it appropriate to use camera enforcement.
The Authority have been on notice from the outset that this was Mr Janssen’s argument, and so cannot be said to have been taken by surprise. They may in their own minds have good reasons why they chose not to follow the Secretary of State’s guidelines at this location. However they have not even summarised those reasons, and so I cannot be satisfied that in exercising their functions in connection with the civil enforcement of an alleged traffic contravention on this occasion the Enforcement Authority
have had regard to the Secretary of State’s guidance.
The Appellants contend that there has been a procedural impropriety on the part of the Authority. “Procedural impropriety” is defined in Regulation 4(5) of the Civil Enforcement of Parking Contraventions (England) Representations and Appeals Regulations as, ” a failure by the enforcement authority to observe any requirement imposed on it by the 2004 Act, by the General Regulations or by these Regulations in relation to the imposition or recovery of a penalty charge or other sum…”
It is a requirement of the TMA (i.e. the 2004 Act) that the Enforcement authority must have regard to the Secretary of State’s guidance . As Icannot be satisfied that this requirement has been met, I find that there has been a procedural impropriety on the part of the Enforcement Authority.
It follows that I must allow this appeal.
[ Mr Janssen will of course appreciate that I have not found as a fact that the Authority have not had regard to the Secretary of State’s guidance. I have allowed the appeal simply because the Authority have not shown that
Statutory Register Entry Page 2 of 3
I certify this to be a true copy of an entry in the register
Mr R Reeve Proper Officer
they have. Should the Authority produce evidence that they have indeed had regard to the guidance, then a future appeal on this basis would not
necessarily succeed.]
Statutory Register Entry Page 3 of 3
Case number 2120101591 Mr Lee Raymond Cocklin vs Westminster city council
In this case in the notice of rejection by the council they said
“it is not a legal requirement of have signs at each individual CCTV camera location. Signs are posted at relevant Controlled Parking Zone (CPZ) signs, alerting drivers to CCTV camera coverage within that zone area”.
Mr Cocklin asked for evidence of these signs. All the council provided was a picture of a controlled parking zone sign but no warning sign of cctv enforcement. the adjudicator allowed the appeal saying
“Where an Authority has a policy, even if it is one that they are not obliged to have by law, the principles of fairness require that they abide by that policy. In this case the implicit position of the Authority is that they will not use camera enforcement in the absence of warning signs. As they have not produced evidence to show that such signs were actually in place, I find that it would breach the duty of fairness owed by the Authority to motorists in general and to Mr Cocklin in particular to enforce this penalty charge notice. On this narrow basis I therefore allow this appeal.

sections.heading: Case number 2110078937 Mr Satish Kumar Sandal
sections.content:
Mr Sandal received a penalty charge notice in the post after having stopped on a Bus Stand.
He appealed on several grounds
Firstly that he only stopped for one second and that wasn’t actually stopping. The adjudicator disagreed.
Secondly that the bus stop is actually disused. The adjudicator agreed but said it was still a bus stand as buses still used the bay to wait.
Thirdly he said that the bus stand sign was at one end of the thick yellow line instead of in the middle of the recessed bay. The adjudicator said that the presence of the thick yellow line meant that a “no stopping” restriction may be in place.
Finally he appealed on the basis that a sign on the adjoining single yellow line indicated that there were no restrictions on a Sunday.
The adjudicator said that “I take the view that this is a situation where the requirement for a “T-Bar” at the end of the yellow line cannot be overlooked because motorists should not be required to rely solely on the thickness of a line to determine where one set of restrictions begins and another begins”
I am not satisfied that the signage was adequatly clear. I am allowing the appeal.”
sections.contentHtml:
Mr Sandal received a penalty charge notice in the post after having stopped on a Bus Stand.

He appealed on several grounds

Firstly that he only stopped for one second and that wasn’t actually stopping. The adjudicator disagreed.

Secondly that the bus stop is actually disused. The adjudicator agreed but said it was still a bus stand as buses still used the bay to wait.

Thirdly he said that the bus stand sign was at one end of the thick yellow line instead of in the middle of the recessed bay. The adjudicator said that the presence of the thick yellow line meant that a “no stopping” restriction may be in place.

Finally he appealed on the basis that a sign on the adjoining single yellow line indicated that there were no restrictions on a Sunday.

The adjudicator said that “I take the view that this is a situation where the requirement for a “T-Bar” at the end of the yellow line cannot be overlooked because motorists should not be required to rely solely on the thickness of a line to determine where one set of restrictions begins and another begins”

I am not satisfied that the signage was adequatly clear. I am allowing the appeal.”
